food_name: Kway Chap — Braised Offal and Broad Rice Sheet Noodles
food_subgroup: Broad flat rice sheet noodles in dark braising broth with pig intestines, pork belly, braised egg, tofu, and pig skin

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Kway Chap as a prepared Singapore hawker dish. No exact FoodOn term covers this Teochew braised offal noodle combination.
ontology.foodex2_description: Broad rice noodle with braised offal and dark soy broth (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Kway Chap)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0KDA used as closest parent for rice noodle dish with meat accompaniments. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this Teochew hawker preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Kway Chap as a prepared hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-stall plate of Kway Chap weighs approximately 560g, comprising flat broad rice sheet noodles (~180g cooked), dark braising broth (~200ml), pig intestines (~60g), pork belly (~40g), braised firm tofu (~40g), braised hard-boiled egg (~1 whole), and pig skin (~20g).

nutrient_source.title: Kway Chap — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for Kway Chap and its component ingredients (broad rice noodles, braised pork belly, pig intestines, braised egg, tofu, pig skin, dark soy broth). Per-100g values calculated by weighted component averaging across the 560g standard plate. Core macros and micronutrients cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (pork intestine, pork belly, firm tofu, hard-boiled egg) where HPB data was supplemented.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice noodles and analogous starchy foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kway Chap as a composite dish. GI ~70 estimated from the closest analogous food: fresh flat rice noodles (fully gelatinised rice starch), which have a reported GI of approximately 61–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The broad, thick rice sheet format of kway results in a higher GI than thin rice vermicelli due to greater starch gelatinisation surface area. Glycemic load per plate calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 52.1) / 100 ≈ 36.5, rounded to 35 consistent with input source. The protein and fat content from braised offal and pork belly components moderates the postprandial glucose response relative to a pure noodle serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,050mg sodium per standard plate (560g), primarily from dark soy master braising stock
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Kway Chap consumption; request reduced dark soy gravy at the hawker stall, avoid drinking the braising broth, and treat this as an occasional meal rather than a regular dietary choice, as a single plate provides over 45% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) and high glycemic load (~35 per plate) from fully gelatinised broad flat rice sheet noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of the kway noodle component — requesting half the noodle portion reduces GL to approximately 17–18 (moderate range) — while the protein-rich offal and pork belly accompaniments do provide some glycemic moderation; blood glucose monitoring post-meal is advisable for insulin-managed patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol from pig intestines (~380mg cholesterol per 100g cooked intestine), high sodium (~1,050mg per plate), and moderate saturated fat (~9.5g per plate)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should avoid or strictly limit Kway Chap due to the combination of high dietary cholesterol from pig intestines, high sodium from the braising broth, and moderate saturated fat from pork belly and pig skin, all of which are adverse for LDL cholesterol management and blood pressure control; if consumed, omit the intestine component and request minimal broth.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High cholesterol from pig intestines clinically relevant for RA patients with concurrent dyslipidaemia; high sodium exacerbates fluid retention in steroid-treated RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients, particularly those on corticosteroids or with concurrent hypercholesterolaemia, should consume Kway Chap in moderation and consider omitting the pig intestine component; regular monitoring of LDL levels is advised for patients who frequently consume offal dishes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kway Chap (粿汁) is a traditional Teochew Chinese hawker dish widely eaten in Singapore and Malaysia, typically served at breakfast, lunch, or dinner at hawker centres. The dish consists of two components served together: the 'Kway' — flat, broad, silky rice sheet noodles (cut from large steamed rice flour sheets) served in a bowl of dark, savoury herbal braising broth (lor) made from dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and Chinese five-spice herbs — and the 'Chap' — a platter of assorted slow-braised accompaniments including pig intestines (large and small), pork belly, braised firm tofu (tau kwan), braised hard-boiled eggs, and pig skin. All accompaniments are slow-braised in a 'master stock' that is replenished daily at traditional stalls. The rice noodle sheets are soft, slippery, and fully gelatinised, giving them a high glycemic index (~70). The dish is known for its complex, earthy, deeply savoury flavour profile and the variety of textures — silky noodles, tender pork belly, chewy intestines, gelatinous pig skin, and firm tofu. A standard hawker plate (~560g) provides approximately 615 kcal, 28g protein, 27g fat, 52g carbohydrate, and ~1,050mg sodium. Clinically, Kway Chap is HIGH concern for hypertension (very high sodium from dark soy broth), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI and GL from broad rice noodles), and cardiovascular disease (high dietary cholesterol from pig intestines ~380mg/100g, moderate saturated fat). The dish is NOT halal (contains pork offal). Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database with component calculation. Confidence: medium-high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database with component-level weighted calculation across the standard 560g plate. Core macros and key micronutrients are well-supported by HPB and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual components. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are estimated from enriched component data with confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for fresh flat rice noodles. No direct laboratory analysis of a composite Kway Chap plate exists in the public domain. Overall confidence: medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Kway Chap dish (including braising broth, pig intestines, pork belly, pig skin, and tofu) is not available as a single entry in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026; individual component fatty acid data could be used for future refinement. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish including broth absorption is not reported in the HPB source. 3. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for this traditional braised preparation per HPB source. 4. Copper (0.08mg/100g), manganese (0.05mg/100g), and vitamin K (0.5mcg/100g) are estimated from enriched component data (confidence 0.9) and are not directly reported in the primary HPB source. 5. GI ~70 is an analogous estimate based on fresh flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for composite Kway Chap exists. 6. Pig intestines contain approximately 380mg cholesterol per 100g cooked — the highest cholesterol contributor in this dish. 7. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork and pork offal). 8. Dietary tags: gluten-free (rice noodles; however, soy sauce may contain wheat — advise coeliac patients to confirm with stall) and dairy-free.
